story:
Neptune’s trailer smells like burnt sugar and borrowed pages while she ices eclipse-blue cupcakes that glitter like small planets. The 29 Palms sky simmers in pink and rust, the desert humming with a warning the wind keeps flipping to in her tarot. A week-old dream told her to bake: “She’s coming. Prep something sweet. You’ll need it when she brings the second sun.” So Neptune works through golden hour, sticking a sparkler in the center cupcake and whispering to the sky to finally keep its promise.

Then the Mojave goes silent, not just quiet—as if it’s holding its breath. On the horizon, a girl in a silver space suit walks the dirt like a runway, helmet tucked beneath an arm, hair matted with stars. She reaches Neptune and says, calm as a homecoming, “I have news. And I think the sun is splitting.” She accepts the cupcake like a sacred thing. “Vanilla,” she says. “You remembered.”

But Neptune doesn’t remember. Not consciously. Still, something in her—like a childhood song—knows. The girl explains she’s been skipping realities like scratched records; one of them cracked. This version called her back. The second sun is in her chest now; it’s what guided her across. A faint glow pulses under silver, a heartbeat with starlight in it.

Inside the trailer, everything buzzes with electric déjà vu. The girl sets down a cassette labeled: For Neptune—Play When the Sky Breaks. Neptune’s thrifted pink Crosley becomes a sort-of portal under the girl’s hands, a hidden dial clicking into place, a slot appearing where there wasn’t one. The tape whirs. A younger Neptune speaks: “If you’re hearing this, it means the loop broke. It means she came back. You have to remember who she is. You have to forgive her. You promised—remember? Before they split the sky?”

“Who are you?” Neptune asks, breathless.

“They used to call me Satellite,” the girl answers, smiling like a crescent. “You called me something else, but I liked that one. It made me feel tethered.” She flips through Neptune’s records with practiced wonder. “Good. You kept the Mars Volta one. That was the key last time. In case the cassette didn’t work.”

Outside, the sky can’t decide which sunset to run. A Joshua tree burns quietly, then reassembles in reverse. Far away, Paramore drifts in as if the desert itself pressed play. Satellite says there isn’t much time. The second sun hums—a low, velvety note that feels like memory taking root. They lace frosting-sticky fingers. The glow from Satellite’s chest paints the walls gold.

Neptune asks what comes next. “We start over,” Satellite says. “We rerun the timeline. This time, you don’t forget.” The desert bows. The mountains blink. A seam opens in the sky, and Neptune sees every version of herself—every loop, every hopeful cupcake, every girl she waited for under the same sun—and in each one, Satellite is always walking toward her.

“I think I’m ready to remember,” Neptune says.

“Good,” Satellite answers, lips brushing Neptune’s temple like a promise kept at last. “This time, we make it stick.”

The cassette rewinds. The second sun brightens. The sky splits cleanly open as Paramore screams something sacred: “That’s what you get when you let your heart win.”
